author: @kevingill9658
text: Just a quick Thank you as I had just been trying to figure out a horizontal turbine to use as I saw being used by another country in the middle of their streets to produce power from the air currents produced by the traffic ( I think it was Spain or France not sure ) anyway as I live on a very busy main road in my town and have been planing to put up a new solid fence It came to me that I could do the same thing. I want to add several of these type turbines in the body of the fence using its structure to include the frame for them. I want to build them thinner than the one you have here, with aluminum blades made from old news paper printing plates, PVC or aluminum body parts to keep the weights to the bare minimums I like the idea of having the magnets on the drum and the Transformer on the base. This should reduce the barrel and fins weight as well Thanks for the ideas. I'm working on a similar idea of my own design and after I get it up and running to show proof of concept I may make another and make a video of my build. Again thanks for the ideas and the videos, I am now a new loyal follower of your channel .

author: @buffplums
text:
As a suggestion, if you produce a series of coils and connect them in series and even,y space them around the circumference.

Also to get the best magnetic coupling you need to have a ferrite core for each coil to increase the amount of magnetic flux in each coil. Just having air spaced coils will give you low saturation and less output. 

Also to gauge the power output you have to take into account that when under load, there will be a significant amount of braking so this is where to get maximum energy it’s a function of the work done, ie proportional to the amount of surface area to the wind versus the braking effect as you load the coils.

Can’t wait to see how it goes but you can improve it all the time, it’s a great project Rob with lots of potential (haha see what I did there.... potential....ok I’ll get me coat) 

Yeah great stuff Rob love seeing all your builds.

author: @sketch2620
text:
Ahh yes, that's smooth.  Your support-structure is nice.  I wonder, and you may have wondered as well:  do you get better performance out of it by tilting those supports at a different angle from 45-degrees?  I'm thinking if there's more room for the air to head in straight-on at the leading side, then there will be less turbulent flow, *much more laminar* in it's approach.  

I hate to mention the swastika symbol, for obvious reasons, but I feel if those support vanes leaned out a bit more toward that direction, you might get higher speeds.  Don't think I would go completely open, but somewhere between there;  60 degrees tends to be an easy angle to reproduce.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I never noticed that mate - to me it lookalike the top of Isengard from lord of the rings - but we all see things differently I guess - also the swastika is a symbol of peace - or is it good luck -  in the Far East - either way not only do we see different things we also have different a social interpretation

author: @Gallardo6669
text: 1kw=wild claim. Typical for hobbyists. Claim nothing worth without proof. I work myself in the wind industry.... These days it's all about pr and false advertisement.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't want to be rude here mate but the wind industry is the worst for wild claims - A commercial turbine claiming 1kW produces on average around 70-100w - I've looked at the power curves and typical wind speeds - don't you think your industry should have some sense of shame? Maybe you should look to your own house rather than trying to belittle folks?

replies.author: @Gallardo6669
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering our power curves currently go up to 1,5megawatt and higher. I have nothing to do with these chinese cheap generators and their well known lying claims. This doesn't represent the wind industry. I mentioned to come from this industry underlining my knowledge about wind turbines, doubting your mentioned output.

author: @windpowerandmore6310
text: I really love your videos! I know you're trying to make that on a budget but I know from experience that you will need a better set of coils for the output you're trying to achieve. When in the amp setting you should have a very hard time to move the magnets past the coil. With that configuration you would need to see a few thousand volts to make 1000 watts. With that type of a rotor you'll never get reasonable rpms. I'm not trying to discourage you,  just giving some advice from 20 plus years of generating my own power.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Nice... I figured  you would have a whole array of microwave transformers. One will not give a significant output. I trust you spaced magnets based on the transformer pole spacing. If you place transformers based on this same spacing output will be in phase with each other and you could simply put them in series or in parallel. I think in this configuration it could do 3W or so. 34 transformers would do 100W maybe. Not the power you are targetting but still an excellent return on your investment.

author: @hatlangston1379
text: Almost all squirrel cage fans are positioned horizontal.  This puts the weight on 2 bearings.  They spin with less resistance and the bearings last longer.  Its fine if you want the vertical axis windmill,  but horizontal might work better.

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: @strongforce8466 people keep suggesting it cause I am sure mr. Smith could make a video to show how it works.  A double half wave rectifier would keep the light on as well but you lose voltage at the diodes. So in reality he just needs to connect half the lights one way and the other half the opposite. Now the two sets alternate.

replies.author: @strongforce8466
replies.text: @MAGnetICus_Attractus or maybe they say it for other people, fair enough

replies.author: @MAGnetICus_Attractus
replies.text: @strongforce8466 one thing I have learned about Robert is he does things his way. He accepts suggestions from others. But he makes the final judgement on how the project is done. Knowing Robert  he did it the quick easy way which is directly connected. Kinda shocked he didn't use a 1000 watt hairdryer to make a few watts with his turbine. One time I got asked why I was using the kirby to spin up one of my disks. So I asked the guy why they use blowers to start jet engines. I was trying to see the wave output of the coil as the magnets spun around. The output from the transformer coil is AC. So the right capacitors are needed and extra diodes. The lights robert is using are diodes. So a second set of lights is the cheaper route. Makes use of both directions of current and could possibly charge a battery to boot. I think a lot of people are seeing the potential this idea has. Myself I noticed how you could see it start cogging when he switched the meter to amps. One of my wind up lights I completely tore up the gearbox when I put a heavy load on it.  I know I put out a suggestion to use a office chair.  Don't  know if Robert read the comment but when he used a office chair I  laughed. What he did wasn't  what I was thinking, but I do like how he used it. Using gutters over cutting pipes in half was another good idea I got from Robert. I know Robert's primary objective on this project is for it to be done as cheap as he can salvaging as much as possible. I think he is doing a great job on his objective. Turning trash into treasure.  Pun intended with turning.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
The more inertia you have, the less problem cogging will offer, but, again, strategic coil-to-magnet ratios [many papers suggest the simple ratio of 2:3 (or 3:2, depending on how you read the information, pretty sure it really does not matter which)] can help greatly reduce the cogging, and this will technically help raise the efficiency of the generator, albeit to what extent, will vary from unit to unit.

I am working [on paper so far] on a way to use a sort of cam to operate the blades at an elevated efficiency, and still yet [HOPEFULLY] keep the overall cost of the unit low enough to justify the added complexity, especially since all such mechanisms must either be carefully concealed from the weather, or else made in such a manner as to make the weather [leaves, twigs, ice, snow, etc] irrelevant. [some sort of low profile design, and/or some form of shielding of the moving parts, especially since in some instances, moving parts could perhaps offer a threat of injury to small animals such as lizards who might accidentally find themselves up inside the cage in between bursts of wind].

While my work has not produced anything conclusive yet, what I do have on paper seems pretty promising, in theory at least.

I must admit, because of the fact you have something where all parts must be mounted and rotated on the same axis, some of these designs are looking like they may need a set of small hidden gears to articulate the cam system, and that indeed would increase the complexity of the machine significantly, so we shall see. ~( ,m,)~

author: @Candyman97
text: Would more transformers generate more power. Like say put 4 on there, just curious. thanks.

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: It will certainly produce more power as more coils are added.

replies.author: @DeliciousDeBlair
replies.text:
Technically [as I pointed out in many previous comments] yes, the potential is there, but you need to also keep in mind that your wind speed will likely have to go up, especially if you do not mount your coils and magnets in such a manner as to reduce the cogging effect.

The point would be to get your magnets and your coils staged in one of the various ratios [many use the simplest one I posted in my long comment of 2:3 or 3:2 ] and this will greatly reduce the resting load required to start your assembly spinning.

Also, while stronger magnets, more windings [more turns per coil], closer magnet-to-coil placement, and increasing coil count will increase the POTENTIAL for more power, you will find yourself at some point reaching a limit where you will simply need more wind force to drive the rotor, so be sure of your base wind supply [the lowest wind you can steadily reply on having where you plan to set up your turbine] is adequate to turn the rotor before committing to it, or else make it a system which can 'cut in and cut out' coils as your load changes.

While this might require a little more complex wiring, and might require a little more skill, or some help, it is one sure-fire way of making the best possible use of what ever wind power you have on hand. (~_^)-b

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it will mate - I could probably fit 20 or so

replies.author: @karlmyers6518
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering looks like it would take as many as you could fit into the space you have .

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: @DeliciousDeBlair There's really no need to cut individual coils in and out. Since the load on the turbine is going to be proportional to the wattage being generated, you can just PWM the combined rectified output to achieve the same effect.
